title: Wrongful Death
description: When a person dies as a result of another party's negligence or wrongful act, surviving family members may have a wrongful death claim. California and Arizona both allow qualified survivors to seek compensation for their loss. This is a deeply sensitive area of law that requires careful, compassionate guidance.
metaTitle: Wrongful Death Claims — What Families Need to Know in CA & AZ
metaDescription: What families should know about wrongful death claims in California and Arizona. Who can file, what damages are available, and when to speak with an attorney.

commonCauses.title: Motor Vehicle Accidents
commonCauses.description: Car, truck, and motorcycle accidents are among the most common causes of wrongful death claims.

commonCauses.title: Workplace Accidents
commonCauses.description: Fatal construction site accidents, industrial accidents, and workplace violence can give rise to wrongful death claims alongside workers' compensation.

commonCauses.title: Medical Malpractice
commonCauses.description: Deaths caused by healthcare provider negligence — including surgical errors, misdiagnosis, and medication errors — may support wrongful death claims.

commonCauses.title: Premises Liability
commonCauses.description: Fatal slip and fall accidents, drownings, and other property-related deaths due to a property owner's negligence.

commonCauses.title: Defective Products
commonCauses.description: Deaths caused by defective vehicles, consumer products, pharmaceuticals, or medical devices.

commonCauses.title: Criminal Acts
commonCauses.description: A wrongful death civil claim can be filed even if criminal charges are also pursued. The standards of proof differ.

immediateSteps.title: Focus on your family's immediate needs first
immediateSteps.description: There is no single right way to respond in the immediate aftermath of losing a loved one. Take care of yourselves and your family before any legal steps.

immediateSteps.title: Preserve all potential evidence
immediateSteps.description: If possible, photograph the scene or preserve any physical evidence related to the death. Evidence lost early can be difficult to reconstruct.

immediateSteps.title: Request the official death certificate
immediateSteps.description: The death certificate is essential documentation for any legal claim and estate matters.

immediateSteps.title: Request a copy of any incident or accident report
immediateSteps.description: Police reports, employer incident reports, and hospital records document the circumstances of the death.

immediateSteps.title: Identify and preserve financial records
immediateSteps.description: Employment records, income documentation, and financial statements establish the economic damages claimable in a wrongful death case.

immediateSteps.title: Consult with a personal injury attorney early
immediateSteps.description: Wrongful death claims involve complex legal standing rules, shorter deadlines in some cases, and coordination with estates. Early consultation is strongly advisable.

immediateSteps.title: Avoid signing anything from an insurer without legal advice
immediateSteps.description: Insurers may approach surviving family members quickly. Do not sign releases, accept settlements, or give recorded statements without consulting an attorney.

immediateSteps.title: Coordinate with the estate
immediateSteps.description: Some wrongful death damages flow through the estate. Understanding how your state's laws coordinate estate and survivor claims is important.

items: Death certificate
Police or incident report
Autopsy or coroner's report
Hospital and emergency records
Any prior medical records relevant to the cause of death
Photos of the accident or incident scene (if accessible)
Witness names and contact information
Names of all potentially liable parties
Insurance policy information for liable parties
Tax returns and W-2s for the deceased (last 2-3 years)
Pay stubs and employment records
Documentation of benefits, pension, or future earning potential
Records of financial support provided to surviving family members
Marriage certificates (for spouse claimants)
Birth certificates (for parent/child claimants)
Evidence of financial dependency
Documentation of the relationship and companionship lost
Any communications from insurers or responsible parties
Any settlement offers received in writing
All correspondence with the deceased's employer if work-related

timelineRisks.action: Do not sign any releases or accept any settlements without consulting an attorney. Early settlements in wrongful death cases are typically far below the full value of the claim.
If a government entity, employee, or vehicle caused the death, California and Arizona have 6-month notice-of-claim requirements. This is a hard deadline.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for wrongful death claims
timelineRisks.action: California's wrongful death statute of limitations is 2 years from the date of death. Missing this deadline typically bars the claim.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for wrongful death claims
timelineRisks.action: Arizona also has a 2-year wrongful death statute of limitations. Consult an attorney promptly to protect the family's rights.

insuranceIssues.issue: Who can file a wrongful death claim
insuranceIssues.explanation: In California, a wrongful death claim may be brought by the surviving spouse, domestic partner, children, or — if none — those who would inherit under intestate succession. In Arizona, similar rules apply. The specifics matter for who receives what.

insuranceIssues.issue: Survival claims vs. wrongful death claims
insuranceIssues.explanation: California allows both a wrongful death claim (for survivors' losses) and a survival action (for the deceased's own pain and suffering and economic losses before death). These are separate and can be pursued together.
Survival claims flow through the deceased's estate. If you are a surviving family member but not the estate representative, coordination is required.

If the death occurred at work, workers' compensation death benefits may be available to dependents. Third-party wrongful death claims may also be available alongside or instead of workers' comp, depending on circumstances.

whenToGetLawyer: A family member died as a result of another party's negligence or wrongful act
An insurer has contacted surviving family members with a settlement offer
The death occurred due to a workplace accident, defective product, or medical error
A government entity may have been involved
You are unsure who has legal standing to file a claim
You need guidance on coordinating a wrongful death claim with the estate
